name: Quadrantids
description: One of the best annual meteor showers, known for bright fireball meteors.
historicalStorms: Consistent high rates, but no major 'storms' in recent history.
historicalVisibility: Best visibility in the Northern Hemisphere; known for bright, long-lasting fireballs.

name: Lyrids
description: One of the oldest known meteor showers, recorded for 2,700 years.
historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres, but better from the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Eta Aquariids
description: Known for their high speed and long glowing trails.
historicalStorms: Consistent activity; Halley's debris produces two annual showers.
historicalVisibility: Best seen from the Southern Hemisphere and tropics.

name: Delta Aquariids
description: Best seen from the southern hemisphere and southern latitudes of the northern hemisphere.
historicalStorms: Steady rates; Machholz is a unique comet with a very small perihelion.
historicalVisibility: Faint meteors, best viewed from the Southern Hemisphere.

name: Perseids
description: The most popular meteor shower of the year, occurring in warm summer nights.
historicalStorms: Major outbursts in 1991-1993 following the parent comet's return.
historicalVisibility: Excellent visibility from the Northern Hemisphere; famous for fireballs.

name: Orionids
description: Fast meteors that often leave persistent trains.
historicalStorms: Enhanced activity periods roughly every 12 years due to Jupiter's influence.
historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres; radiant is near the celestial equator.

name: Leonids
description: Known for spectacular meteor storms every 33 years.
historicalStorms: Famous storms in 1833, 1866, 1966, and 1999-2002 with thousands of meteors per hour.
historicalVisibility: Best seen from the Northern Hemisphere; radiant rises after midnight.

name: Geminids
description: The strongest and most reliable annual meteor shower.
historicalStorms: Consistently increasing in intensity over the last century.
historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres; best in the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Ursids
description: A minor shower that often produces unexpected bursts of activity.
historicalVisibility: Only visible from the Northern Hemisphere; radiant is circumpolar.
